data.title: Grupo Aeroportuario announces credit line refinancing for $40M
data.description:
• Grupo Aeroportuario del Pacífico (NYSE:PAC) announced on Thursday the refinancing of a USD $40M credit line for a five-year term with the same financial institution. 
  • Interest will be payable monthly at a variable rate equivalent to SOFR plus 81 basis points, with no additional commissions. 
  • The payment of the principal will be due at maturity, on September 18, 2030.

data.title: AvePoint announces dual listing on the Singapore exchange
data.description:
•  AvePoint (NASDAQ:AVPT) announced on Thursday its listing on the Main Board of the Singapore Exchange Securities Trading Limited under the symbol “AVP.” 
  • AvePoint established its initial presence in Singapore in 2009, and Singapore now serves as the company’s Asia Headquarters and International R&D Hub.

data.title: Japan's inflation eases to 2.7% in August
data.description:
• Japan's annual inflation rate eased to 2.7% in August 2025 from 3.1% in the previous month, marking the lowest reading since October 2024.
  • The slowdown was driven mainly by more significant drops in electricity prices, thanks to government subsidies, and a fall in gas prices.
  • Inflation accelerated for housing, clothing, transport, communications, and miscellaneous goods.
  • On the food side, prices rose 7.2%, easing from July’s five-month peak of 7.6%, driven by the smallest gain in rice prices in eight months amid Tokyo’s ongoing efforts to curb staple food costs.
  • Core inflation, which excludes fresh food but includes energy, also stood at 2.7% annually, matching market expectations and reaching a nine-month low.
  • On a monthly basis, the CPI edged up 0.1%, holding steady for the third straight month.

data.title: Reviva Pharmaceuticals announces pricing of $9M public offering
data.description:
• Reviva Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:RVPH) announced on Thursday a public offering with existing and new healthcare-focused institutional investors for the purchase and sale of 27M shares of its common stock together with Series E warrants and Series F warrants, at a combined offering price of $0.335 per share, for gross proceeds of approximately $9M.  
  • The Series E Warrants and the Series F Warrants will have an exercise price of $0.335 per share. 
  • The company currently intends to use the net proceeds from the offering to fund research and development activities and for working capital and other general corporate purposes.

data.title: Ventas declares $0.48 dividend
data.description:
• Ventas (NYSE:VTR) declares $0.48/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 2.82%
  • Payable Oct. 16; for shareholders of record Sept. 30; ex-div Sept. 30.
  • See VTR Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count 
 
The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs. Used for drilling wellbores for wells that may eventually produce oil or gas, active rigs are essential for the exploration and development of oil and gas fields. 
 
2:30 PM Fed's Daly Speech 
 
San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will participate in fireside chat on AI implications for workforce development and economic mobility hosted by the Emerging Tech Economic Research Network.

data.title: CDC vaccines panel votes to change recommendation for MMRV vaccine for children
data.description:
The CDC's Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices, in which five of its 12 members were just added earlier this week, voted to change an existing recommendation that allows administering the measles, mumps, rubella, and varicella (chickenpox) shot to children under the age of four.

The panel voted 8-3 vote -- there was one abstention -- that the vaccine should not be given to those under four years old. 

Several members appeared to be swayed by data indicating there was a slight increase in the risk of fever-related seizures in children one to two years old after receiving the MMRV jab compared to the MMR one, which doesn't include chickenpox.

About 85% of children in the US get separate vaccines for MMR and chickenpox, according to NPR. The MMR vaccine schedule is not impacted by the committee's vote.   

However, in a separate vote, the panel voted 8-1 that the Vaccines for Children program, which provide shots to children from poor families, should still cover the MMRV vaccine.

Both votes are not binding and a final decision will be made by acting CDC director Jim O'Neill, who is also deputy secretary at HHS, making him close to HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. 

The committee was also supposed to vote on changes to hepatitis B vaccine recommendations, but ran out of time due to member debate. It will now come on Friday. The current stance is to administer the vaccine to all babies when they are born, irrespective of the mother's hepatitis B status. It could be changed to only recommend it if a mother does not test negative for the disease. 

Also on Friday, the panel will vote on potential changes to recommendations for COVID-19 vaccines. 

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: FedEx targets $17.20–$19 EPS for FY26 with $1B transformation savings amid global trade headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: FedEx Corporation (FDX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Rajesh Subramaniam, President, CEO & Director, stated that "We delivered a solid quarter in line with the Q1 outlook we shared in June, despite significant volatility and uncertainty around the global trade environment." The company emphasized continued progress on reducing structural costs, deploying the Tricolor strategy, advancing Network 2.0, and preparing for the spin-off of FedEx Freight, which remains on track.
  • Subramaniam highlighted, "Revenue was up 3% year-over-year, driven by strength across our U.S. domestic package services. We achieved our targeted $200 million in transformation-related savings and grew adjusted operating income by 7%." He also noted winning new business in high-value verticals and strong digital tools, and provided an update that "our next FedEx Corp. Investor Day will be held in Memphis on February 11 and 12, 2026."
  • Brie Carere, Executive VP & Chief Customer Officer, reported a 5% increase in U.S. domestic ADV year-over-year and noted, "Best Buy names FedEx as their primary national parcel carrier."
  • John Dietrich, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "On a consolidated basis, in the first quarter, we delivered $3.83 in adjusted earnings per share, up 6% year-over-year...We grew adjusted operating income by approximately $90 million despite the $150 million headwind from the global trade environment, $130 million of headwind from the U.S. Postal Service contract expiration and continued softness at FedEx Freight."

Outlook

  • Management expects full year adjusted earnings to be $17.20 to $19 per diluted share. This guidance reflects a range of scenarios in a dynamic global operating environment, with focus on delivering $1 billion in transformation-related savings and executing commercial priorities.
  • Carere elaborated, "we are currently planning for revenue growth of 4% to 6%. The top of this range assumes that current favorable trends in the U.S. Domestic segment continue, and the lower end assumes incremental pressure on U.S. demand."
  • Dietrich indicated, "At the midpoint of our range, we anticipate a 6% increase in Federal Express revenue with adjusted operating margin down slightly. Also at the midpoint, we expect low single-digit improvement in FedEx Freight revenue with margin down year-over-year."

Financial Results

  • Dietrich reported, "In the first quarter, we delivered $3.83 in adjusted earnings per share, up 6% year-over-year."
  • Consolidated revenue was up 3%. Adjusted operating income grew by 7%, and adjusted operating margin expanded by 20 basis points.
  • FEC revenue increased 4%, with adjusted operating income up 17% and adjusted operating margin expanded by 70 basis points.
  • At FedEx Freight, adjusted operating income declined by just over $70 million and adjusted operating margin contracted 250 basis points.
  • The company purchased $500 million in stock during the quarter and has $1.6 billion remaining under its 2024 stock repurchase authorization.
  • Q1 CapEx was $623 million, with a target of $4.5 billion in annual CapEx for FY26.

Q&A

  • Jordan Alliger, Goldman Sachs: Asked about factors influencing the EPS range. Dietrich responded that the outcome will be determined by variables including global trade, industrial economy, and U.S. domestic demand.
  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: Queried about cost headwinds. Dietrich confirmed, “there will be pressure...$1 billion of headwind as a result of some of the environmental impacts.” Subramaniam added, “We're doing everything in our power to make sure that we can improve our customer experience and mitigate the costs as we move forward.”
  • Bascome Majors, Susquehanna: Asked about data strategy. Subramaniam explained the value of FedEx’s data, emphasizing, "The important thing is that we started this work back in 2020...it's already bearing fruit in our operations as deep learning models are enhancing our predictability."
  • Multiple analysts pressed on global trade headwinds, specifically the $1 billion impact and its composition. Carere clarified, “that $1 billion of headwind is predominantly an impact of top line revenue reduction because China to the U.S. is a very profitable lane for us.”
  • Questions on domestic volume outlook, competitive landscape, and new business wins were addressed by Carere, who noted momentum in SMB and health care segments, and that pricing and market share gains are expected to continue.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a slightly negative tone, with repeated probing around the impact and clarity of global trade headwinds, operating leverage, and guidance bridge. Several questions sought more granular explanations for cost pressures and margin dynamics.
  • Management’s tone was confident in prepared remarks, frequently citing resilience and execution, but during Q&A, responses included defensive and clarifying language such as “as I said before” and “we intend to deliver on and we'll be focused on staying in the range,” indicating some defensiveness and caution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism increased and management appeared more cautious when discussing the outlook and global trade impacts.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance in Q1 2026 is more specific, providing a full-year EPS range and revenue growth targets, versus the prior quarter which only offered Q1 guidance due to uncertainty.
  • Strategic focus on digital transformation, AI, and the upcoming FedEx Freight spin-off continued, but Q1 remarks highlighted more pronounced global trade headwinds and the impact of the de minimis exemption removal.
  • Management confidence remained strong in prepared remarks, but the Q&A reflected heightened caution and a need to repeatedly explain cost headwinds.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted more intensely to understanding the $1 billion global trade impact and operating leverage, indicating greater concern versus the prior quarter’s focus on transformation progress and initial Freight spin-off plans.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted a $1 billion headwind from the global trade environment, mainly due to the removal of the de minimis exemption and reduced China–U.S. export volumes.
  • Additional risks include incremental customs clearance costs and persistent softness in the industrial economy.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the potential for further pressure on margins and the sustainability of domestic volume growth.
  • Management is mitigating risks through ongoing cost reductions, network flexibility, and a disciplined approach to revenue quality and capital allocation.

Final Takeaway

FedEx management underscored its ability to deliver solid performance in a challenging global trade environment, with a focus on disciplined cost management, digital innovation, and network optimization. The company reiterated its FY26 adjusted EPS guidance of $17.20 to $19, supported by $1 billion in transformation-related savings, ongoing onboarding of major clients, and continued investment in technology and operational efficiency, while acknowledging persistent headwinds from global trade and U.S. Postal Service contract expiration as key variables for the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Scholastic affirms 2%–4% revenue growth guidance while advancing digital IP expansion and real estate monetization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Scholastic Corporation (SCHL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Peter Warwick, President, CEO & Director, stated that "Scholastic had a productive summer, as we prepared for the back-to-school season and advanced important initiatives. As expected, our first quarter reflected the normal seasonality of our business, with an operating loss in line with previous years." He emphasized significant investor interest in the company's SoHo headquarters and Jefferson City distribution center as part of the ongoing real estate monetization process, noting, "We remain on track, with the time line, we outlined in July."
  • Warwick highlighted the company's ongoing push for financial discipline and operational leverage, while affirming full year guidance and focusing on profitable growth. He referenced strong trade sales in the Children's Book Publishing and Distribution segment, reporting that "Suzanne Collins: Sunrise on the reaping, has now sold 3.7 million copies worldwide, since its March release," and previewed several major upcoming releases, including new titles in the I Survived and Dog Man series.
  • Warwick detailed the integration of trade, fairs, and clubs into the new Children's Book group, targeting cost savings and streamlined operations. He further announced the launch of the first-ever Scholastic branded streaming app with Future Today and noted, "We expect to surpass 10 million monthly views by calendar year-end, of classic Clifford content on YouTube."
  • In Scholastic Education, Warwick acknowledged sales pressure due to "a volatile funding environment, reflecting the delay of some federal education grants and cancellation of others," but affirmed confidence in the segment's long-term potential under new leadership and a rationalized product focus.
  • Haji Glover, CFO & Executive Vice President, stated, "Revenues decreased 5% to $225.6 million. Our seasonally adjusted operating loss improved to $81.9 million from $85.6 million, in the prior year period. Reflecting cost-saving initiatives, adjusted EBITDA was a loss of $55.7 million, an improvement from a loss of $60.5 million a year ago. Net loss was $63.3 million compared to $60.3 million, in the prior year period." Glover also noted, "Book fair revenue were $34.1 million in the quarter, an increase of 18%, driven by higher Scholastic dollar redemptions."

Outlook

  • The company is "affirming our fiscal year 2026 guidance for revenue growth of 2% to 4%. Adjusted EBITDA of $160 million to $170 million and full year free cash flow between $30 million and $40 million." Management cited expectations for a strong second quarter, supported by major trade releases, and ongoing cost optimization, including an expected $10 million in incremental tariff expenses this year.
  • Warwick stated, "We remain confident in our ability to deliver meaningful profit growth, while continuing to create long-term value for our shareholders and lasting impact for children worldwide."

Financial Results

  • Glover reported, "In Children's Book Publishing and Distribution, revenues for the first quarter increased 4% to $109.4 million, reflecting growth in school book fairs. Segment adjusted operating loss improved to $34.3 million from $36.6 million in the prior year period."
  • Book Clubs revenue was $1.8 million compared to $2.7 million a year ago, attributed to mailing timing. Trade Publishing revenue was $73.5 million, flat year-over-year, with strong demand for popular franchises. Scholastic Education segment revenues were $40.1 million versus $55.7 million in the prior year, with segment adjusted operating loss at $21.2 million compared to a loss of $17 million previously. Entertainment segment revenues fell to $13.6 million from $16.6 million, with a segment adjusted operating loss of $4 million.
  • International segment revenues were $59.4 million, up from $56.8 million, with segment adjusted operating results improving to a loss of $4.1 million from $8.3 million. Unallocated overhead costs decreased by $6.6 million to $18.3 million, mainly due to employee expense reductions.
  • Seasonal net cash used by operating activities was $81.8 million compared to $41.9 million, with free cash used at $100.2 million versus $68.7 million in the prior year. Borrowings stood at $325 million and net debt at $242.8 million.

Q&A

  • Brendan Michael McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC, inquired about early feedback from new Education Solutions products and their market resonance. Jeffrey Mathews responded that "we were getting great feedback from customers around some of the new products... we are very encouraged by the -- what we're hearing particularly with knowledge library and as well as our core products, our classroom libraries and our classroom magazines."
  • McCarthy asked about variables influencing a turnaround in education spending. Mathews explained that "as there's greater funding certainty and we've seen that some of the federal programs that had been paused or some federal grants have been paused were released in late August. As there becomes more certainty, we expect that school district and -- school and district leaders will be more forthcoming."
  • McCarthy queried about the Entertainment segment's digital strategy. Warwick responded, "The digital model that we now have and the digital income that we're getting is high margin and it's going to grow... we will see the major benefits, going progressively out into the future."
  • On monetizing digital content, Glover stated, "we're really in early stages of this... we're only on 2 platforms with the opportunity to increase that to another 6 or 7 platforms... being able to actually quantify this impact is probably going to take us a few months."
  • Questions about cost structure were addressed by Glover: "We did -- we definitely took a really good look at it prior to actually given our guidance, and our guidance reflects the majority of our spend reductions. I think we announced somewhere between $15 million and $20 million of price cost reductions."
  • On guidance sensitivity, Glover highlighted potential retail market variability and tariff costs: "...the most important thing for us is the concerns of tariffs, as it reflects our business because we are a retail business. And those expenses, which we've already planned for, which is about $10 million this year, we're continuing to monitor all the things that are going on with the government, down in D.C."
  • An unknown analyst asked about the Education Solutions trajectory, and Mathews said, "we are expecting, based on the current patterns that this year will be more back-end loaded than previously... we also expect that... those headwinds will moderate over the fall and into the spring."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions focused on cost control, education funding uncertainty, and the pace of digital monetization, with a generally neutral to slightly cautious tone reflecting industry headwinds and timing uncertainties.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, particularly regarding digital strategy and cost savings, using phrases such as "we are pretty confident" and "I'm extremely excited and confident about where we are."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady, with continued optimism about cost reductions and strategic initiatives. Analysts' tone did not shift significantly, with ongoing focus on structural challenges and forward-looking risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 was affirmed at 2% to 4% revenue growth, $160 million to $170 million in adjusted EBITDA, and $30 million to $40 million in free cash flow, unchanged from the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus advanced on digital content and IP monetization, with launches like the Scholastic streaming app and greater YouTube integration, compared to a prior focus on organizational restructuring and integration.
  • Cost reduction efforts continued as a major theme, with the current quarter emphasizing realized savings and ongoing SG&A optimization. Analysts' questions persisted around education funding and digital content profitability, mirroring concerns raised previously.
  • Key metrics such as operating loss and cash usage remained aligned with historical seasonality, and management's confidence in achieving guidance was reiterated.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited a "volatile funding environment" and delays in federal education grants as near-term headwinds for the Education segment.
  • Ongoing tariff expenses were estimated at $10 million for the fiscal year, and management indicated active monitoring of trade policy developments.
  • Cash flow was pressured by higher inventory purchases, timing of expense payments, and increased interest, though mitigated by cost-saving initiatives and share buybacks.
  • Uncertainty in retail and school spending, as well as the pace of digital revenue recognition, were noted as potential variables impacting guidance.

Final Takeaway

Scholastic began fiscal 2026 with continued progress on cost discipline, IP-driven digital expansion, and real estate monetization initiatives, while affirming its full-year targets for revenue growth, profitability, and free cash flow. Management emphasized confidence in the company's back-to-school momentum, robust publishing pipeline, and ongoing strategic transformation to drive long-term value for shareholders and educational communities alike.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Exxon seeks Trump administration's help in thwarting European Union climate law
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) called Thrusday for European leaders to repeal a new climate law that would fine corporations, with CEO Darren Woods saying the directive's penalties of as much as 5% of a company's global revenue would be "bone-crushing."

The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive, which requires companies to fix human rights and environmental issues within their supply chains or face large fines and was adopted last year, is "the worst piece of legislation I've seen since I've been in this job," Woods told Bloomberg in an interview. "Given the perspective I have around the world, that says quite a bit."

In response to criticism that the law risks harming the continent's competitiveness, the European Commission proposed a set of changes earlier this year to loosen the requirements, but Woods said it would not be enough, calling for the law to be revoked entirely.

The directive could require all large businesses in Europe to develop legally binding plans to comply with the Paris Agreement climate treaty, and "opens up standing for any activist or NGO who wants to challenge those plans and take us to court," the CEO said.

Woods said he has spoken about the regulation with President Trump and other members of the administration, which has raised concerns about the directive as part of trade negotiations with the block.

Exxon (NYSE:XOM) has been "slowly been pulling out of Europe," Woods said, noting the company has shut down or exited 19 operations because of what he said was red tape that hurts business.
